title: 🎙️ Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling' — how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Most genuine arts/culture/media journalism should land well above 0, even without a Cariboo tie-in:
- 80-100: Cariboo/Williams Lake-specific arts and culture news — community arts council grants or murals, local festivals, Indigenous film/storytelling, community theatre, museum/heritage programs, a Cariboo author or artist receiving an award, community radio or local publishing.
- 55-79: Rural BC or Canadian arts/culture journalism without a specific Cariboo tie — CBC Arts features on Indigenous storytelling, regional festivals, community media projects, heritage preservation, creative-economy stories in small towns, digital storytelling tools and platforms.
- 30-54: General arts, culture, media, or entertainment journalism with real creative/storytelling substance but no rural or community angle (national awards shows, mainstream film/TV/music industry coverage, publishing industry news).
- 15-29: Tangentially creative content (a tech feature that touches on media/publishing/streaming) or local Cariboo civic news with no arts/culture angle.
- 0-14: No arts, culture, media, or storytelling dimension at all.

Strong fits include: community arts journalism (arts councils, local festivals, heritage programs), oral history and cultural preservation projects, Indigenous film and storytelling, community theatre and local media, rural arts funding and the creative economy in small towns, and digital storytelling platforms and tools. Also strong fits: community media tech (podcasting, streaming, local publishing), creative tech economy, open-source arts tools, Cariboo arts festivals using digital outreach, animation/film/media production in rural BC, local radio/podcast infrastructure, and how rural communities use technology for creative expression. A story about a Cariboo community mural, a CBC Arts feature on BC Indigenous storytelling, a rural arts council receiving grants, or a Cariboo author/artist shortlisted for an award is a strong fit even without an explicit technology angle — cultural journalism matters to this audience. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): medical and health research with no cultural/storytelling angle; working lands and resource economy stories with no cultural framing; wildlife and nature stories without creative or community media context; data centre infrastructure and AI investment news; geopolitical conflict, war, and crime coverage; financial market and stock movement stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] Outrage as woman jailed for three years after criticising Somali government online
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Sentencing of 27-year-old Sadia Moalim Ali condemned by former president and prime ministers as well as rights groups

A rickshaw driver in Somalia has been sentenced to three years in prison for comments she made on social media, in a case that has caught the public’s attention and provoked outrage in the country.

Sadia Moalim Ali, a 27-year-old nursing graduate, was originally charged with insulting government institutions and incitement to commit a crime, but convicted only of the former. Her sentence, immediately condemned as “fundamentally unjust”, was handed down on 25 June.

Continue reading...

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Convent of Santa Maria de Mave in Santa María de Mave, Spain
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

 This building is in the village of Santa Maria  de Mave ,  The village takes  name from the church and convento at its centre and is close to the larger settlement of Mave close to the border of  Cantabria and Castile y Leòn .

In Spain the term  Convento is used to distinguish the institution  as housing a group of religious members of an order which is active in the community  in which it is based as opposed to a contemplative, isolated  order in which case the building would be termed a monestery. To a certain  extent the terms are interchangeable  and the term "convent" certainly  does not imply that the residents were nuns. In this case the occupants were male friars. The term Monastario is often applied nowadays to the church building directly attached to the Convento with the Romanesque church being in its original religious form (with with a triple apse and three naves,  a bell wall, currently with resident white storks, and a tall dome over the transept). The Convento has been converted from the cloister (rebuilt in the 18th century) to a boutique hotel with a restaurant  and a bar/cafeteria. The conversion  has been done sympathetically with many original feature retained but the architect clearly has a love for the modern. Some of the bedrooms are actually in the monk's cells.

The origins of the Convento and the associated church date as far back as the second half of the 9th century. The abbey was founded by King Alphonse III . In 1033, Mave and its possessions became part of the important Benedictine monastery of San Salvador de Oña, in Burgos. Nothing remains of the ancient Pre-Romanesque structure.
items.summary: This building is in the village of Santa Maria de Mave , The village takes name from the church and convento at its centre and is close to the larger settlement of Mave close to the border of Cantabria and Castile y Leòn . In Spain the term Convento is used to distinguish the institution as housing

items.title: [Boing Boing] Kevin Mitnick left the man who helped jail him enough for a Porsche 911
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Kevin Mitnick, the legendary hacker, once tried to social-engineer his way into Novell's network by phoning a network admin named Shawn Nunley late at night, posing as an employee who needed "direct inbound modem access" for a top-secret project.

Nunley smelled a rat, played along, and asked the caller to leave a voicemail — which he recorded onto a cassette recorder. — Read the rest

The post Kevin Mitnick left the man who helped jail him enough for a Porsche 911 appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [New Atlas] High-sensitivity electro snout sniffs out unsafe food
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Though most human noses can detect suspicious smells at relatively high levels, they're not always able to discern every single scent. Researchers from the University of California - Berkeley, however, have developed an “electronic nose” that can pick up on the gases emitted by expired food or food allergens which they claim is “better than human noses.”

Continue Reading

Category: Technology

Tags: UC Berkeley, Allergies, Electronic Nose, Food technology, Nanotubes

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Astronomers found two rare super puff planets lighter than cotton candy
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Two newly confirmed "super-puff" planets are so diffuse that they are less dense than cotton candy, despite being about the size of Jupiter. Their rare orbital relationship and enormous, lightweight atmospheres could provide valuable clues about how some of the strangest planets in the galaxy come to exist.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Massive study links teen marijuana use to double the risk of serious mental illness
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Teens who use cannabis may face a substantially greater risk of developing serious mental health conditions, including psychotic and bipolar disorders, according to a study of more than 463,000 adolescents. Researchers found cannabis use often preceded these diagnoses by nearly two years, strengthening concerns about its long-term effects on developing brains.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] Trafficked, beaten and raped: raids reveal scale of abuse of women in Asia’s cyberscam centres
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

As tens of thousands are freed, female survivors are increasingly reporting gender-based violence in the compounds, previously thought to hold mainly men

Late one evening in October 2023, Sarah* felt labour pains starting. It was 11pm, but at the cyberscam compound inside Laos’ Golden Triangle, workers were logging on for a long night shift, scamming Americans online.

Every night, workers sat at their computers until the early hours, building fake profiles of glamorous, jet-setting women on Facebook and Instagram. Sarah trawled the web to find older men to target with messages, where she fawned over their jobs, asked how their day had been and exchanged photos of luxury travel and beach trips. Each conversation she had was meticulously designed to follow a multi-day script, and monitored by bosses who walked up and down the long rows of desks.

Continue reading...

items.content_html:

Transit 2 min.read # Summer traffic in the Kootenays increases risk for roadside workers My East Kootenay Now Staff By My East Kootenay Now Staff June 28, 2026 FacebookXReddItWhatsAppEmail With the July 1 holiday marking the start of the busy summer travel season, drivers across the Kootenays will encounter more orange cones, construction crews and roadside workers as highways and local roads see increased traffic. Whether heading to a campground, lake, hiking trail or ferry, drivers face added pressure from busy schedules, road delays and long trips.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Coal-Fired Fish and Chip Shop in Upton, England
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

In England fried fish and chips is recognised  by most as the national  takeout dish (with curries being the only real competitor). Nowadays  fish and chips are most commonly fried in vegetable oil (partly because  of perceived health benefits) and, whilst frying in traditional beef dripping is not uncommon, doing so on a traditional coal fired fish and chip range takes place in only around half a dozen places in the country. This rather unassuming shop, in a tiny, brick built lock-up, in the tiny village of Upton, near Gainsborough  in Lincolnshire  is one of them.

It only opens on Friday evenings and Saturday  lunchtimes (this has been the case for many years). These amazing, traditionally cooked, fish and chips are fried in a coal fired range manufactured  in 1947 (according  to the proprietor, and contrary to the 1948 date in the literature) by Frank Ford of Halifax, Yorkshire (still in business but no longer making coal fired ranges) when the shop started trading. At that time coal fired ranges were the most popular  for fish and chip shops but they have largely been replaced by gas and electrically heated ranges.

Whether  because of the heritage  value of the shop or because  of the excellent products queues are common sometimes reaching well down the street with various lamp posts in the street known by the locals as being the 15 minute,30 minute and 1 hour markers for the line.
items.summary: In England fried fish and chips is recognised by most as the national takeout dish (with curries being the only real competitor). Nowadays fish and chips are most commonly fried in vegetable oil (partly because of perceived health benefits) and, whilst frying in traditional beef dripping is not
